bytes.topic: LLM Fundamentals — tokens, context window, temperature, top-p, sampling strategies
bytes.title: anthropic_api — Tokens, Context Windows, and Sampling Parameters Demystified
bytes.body:
Claude models on the Anthropic API expose three key sampling levers. `temperature` (0–1) scales the logit distribution before softmax — 0 collapses it to greedy decoding, 1 leaves it maximally stochastic. `top_p` (nucleus sampling) trims the vocabulary to the smallest set whose cumulative probability exceeds the threshold; `top_p=0.9` means the model samples only from tokens covering 90% of the probability mass. Uniquely, Anthropic also exposes `top_k`, which hard-caps the candidate pool by count — when both are set, they apply in sequence.

Context window: claude-sonnet-4-6 and claude-opus-4-6 support 200k input tokens. Use `client.messages.count_tokens()` to audit prompts before sending — it returns the exact token count the API will charge, preventing silent context overflow. `max_tokens` is a hard output ceiling, not a target; Claude stops earlier at a natural end. Always inspect `response.usage`: it surfaces `cache_read_input_tokens` and `cache_creation_input_tokens` alongside standard counts when prompt caching is active, letting you measure real cache hit rates.
bytes.code_snippet:
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Audit token cost before sending
count = client.messages.count_tokens(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain attention mechanisms."}]
)
print(f"Estimated input tokens: {count.input_tokens}")

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=512,
    temperature=0.3,   # Low entropy for factual output
    top_p=0.9,         # Nucleus sampling threshold
    top_k=40,          # Applied after top_p filter
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain attention mechanisms."}]
)

print(response.content[0].text)
print(response.usage)  # includes cache_read_input_tokens if caching is active

bytes.topic: Prompt Engineering — system prompts, few-shot examples, chain of thought, structured output
bytes.title: Anthropic API Prompt Engineering — System Prompts, Few-Shot, CoT, and Structured Output
bytes.body:
The Anthropic API's `system` parameter is a first-class field — not a prefixed user message. Pass your persona, constraints, and output format there, keeping the `messages` array clean for dialogue. Few-shot examples belong in the `messages` array as alternating `user`/`assistant` turns before your real query; Claude treats them as demonstrated behavior, not instructions.

For chain-of-thought on claude-sonnet-4-6 or opus-4-6, enable extended thinking via `thinking: { type: 'enabled', budget_tokens: 5000 }`. The response includes a `thinking` content block you can log separately — never surface it raw to end users. For structured output, skip prompt-hacking and use the `tools` parameter: define a single tool whose `input_schema` is your target JSON shape, then set `tool_choice: { type: 'tool', name: 'your_tool' }` to force a call. Claude fills the schema reliably without escape-hatch prose.

bytes.topic: Hugging Face — model hub, transformers library, pipelines, tokenizers
bytes.title: Anthropic API + Hugging Face — Building a Hybrid RAG Pipeline Without Paying for Embeddings
bytes.body: When building RAG systems with Claude, you don't have to choose between Hugging Face's model ecosystem and Anthropic's generation quality — they compose cleanly. HF's `sentence-transformers` models handle embedding and retrieval locally at zero API cost; `client.messages.create()` handles synthesis with Claude's reasoning depth. The pattern: embed your corpus with `all-MiniLM-L6-v2`, retrieve top-k chunks via cosine similarity, then inject them into the `user` message. Claude's 200k context window means you can pass substantial retrieved content without aggressive chunking. For preprocessing, HF pipelines add real leverage — run zero-shot classification or NER before deciding what to retrieve, then pass only high-signal context. Avoid dumping raw HF pipeline outputs directly into Claude; filter by confidence threshold first to avoid poisoning the context with low-signal extractions. For adaptive pipelines, expose retrieval as a `tool` in `tools=[...]` and let Claude decide when to invoke it via `tool_use` content blocks — this eliminates unnecessary retrievals on questions Claude can answer directly from its weights.
bytes.code_snippet:
from transformers import pipeline
import anthropic
import numpy as np

embedder = pipeline("feature-extraction", model="sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2", truncation=True)
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def embed(text: str) -> np.ndarray:
    vecs = embedder(text)[0]  # shape: (tokens, 384)
    return np.mean(vecs, axis=0)

def retrieve(query: str, corpus: list[str], corpus_embs: list, top_k=3) -> list[str]:
    q = embed(query)
    scores = [np.dot(q, e) / (np.linalg.norm(q) * np.linalg.norm(e)) for e in corpus_embs]
    return [corpus[i] for i in np.argsort(scores)[-top_k:][::-1] if scores[i] > 0.4]

context = "\n\n".join(retrieve("How does attention work?", corpus, corpus_embeddings))
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-6",
    max_tokens=512,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Context:\n{context}\n\nAnswer concisely."}]
)

bytes.topic: LangChain — chains, agents, tools, memory, LCEL
bytes.title: Anthropic API + LangChain LCEL — Tool-Calling Chains with Claude
bytes.body:
LangChain's `ChatAnthropic` wraps Claude's Messages API, giving you LCEL-native access to tool calling, streaming, and structured output. The key primitive is `bind_tools()` — it serializes your Python functions into Anthropic's tool schema format and attaches them to every request automatically.

LCEL pipes (`|`) compose Claude into full chains without callbacks or subclassing. A `prompt | llm_with_tools` chain passes `HumanMessage` and `SystemMessage` directly to `claude-opus-4-6`, and tool call results surface as `AIMessage.tool_calls` — a list of dicts with `name`, `args`, and `id`. Feed those back via `ToolMessage` to complete the agentic loop.

For memory, wrap your chain in `RunnableWithMessageHistory` and supply a `BaseChatMessageHistory` store — LangChain injects the history buffer into each Claude request automatically. Skip `ConversationBufferMemory` (legacy); use `InMemoryChatMessageHistory` or a Redis-backed store instead.

The `@tool` decorator auto-generates the JSON schema Claude expects directly from your docstring and type hints — no manual schema authoring needed. Pair with `with_structured_output()` when you need Claude to return a typed Pydantic model instead of raw tool calls.
bytes.code_snippet:
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.tools import tool
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.messages import ToolMessage

@tool
def get_weather(city: str) -> str:
    """Get current weather for a city."""
    return f"{city}: 72°F, sunny"

llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-opus-4-6", temperature=0)
llm_with_tools = llm.bind_tools([get_weather])

chain = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a helpful assistant."),
    ("human", "{input}"),
]) | llm_with_tools

response = chain.invoke({"input": "What's the weather in Tokyo?"})
print(response.tool_calls)
# [{'name': 'get_weather', 'args': {'city': 'Tokyo'}, 'id': 'toolu_...'}]

bytes.topic: LlamaIndex — document loading, indexing strategies, query engines, retrieval
bytes.title: anthropic_api + LlamaIndex — Wiring Claude into Document Indexing and Query Engines
bytes.body:
LlamaIndex's `Anthropic` LLM adapter routes synthesis through Claude, replacing the default OpenAI calls. Wire it in globally via `Settings.llm` and every downstream component — query engines, chat engines, sub-question decomposers — inherits it automatically.

For document loading, `SimpleDirectoryReader` handles local files; swap to LlamaHub loaders for Notion, S3, or PostgreSQL sources. `VectorStoreIndex.from_documents()` chunks docs using `Settings.chunk_size` (default 1024 tokens), embeds them, and builds a retrievable in-memory index. Persist to `pgvector` via `PGVectorStore` for production durability.

At query time, `as_query_engine()` controls two critical levers: `similarity_top_k` (chunks retrieved) and `response_mode` (how Claude synthesizes them). `compact` concatenates all chunks into one Claude call — cheapest. `tree_summarize` fans out multiple Claude calls and recursively merges — best for large corpora. `refine` updates a running answer over each chunk sequentially — highest fidelity, most tokens.

Claude's 200k context window means you can push `similarity_top_k` to 20+ before hitting limits — unlike shorter-context models, `tree_summarize` is rarely needed. Default to `compact` and tune `chunk_overlap` (default 200 tokens) to preserve cross-chunk context.
bytes.code_snippet:
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader, Settings
from llama_index.llms.anthropic import Anthropic

Settings.llm = Anthropic(
    model="claude-opus-4-6",
    api_key="sk-ant-..."
)
Settings.chunk_size = 512
Settings.chunk_overlap = 64

docs = SimpleDirectoryReader("./data").load_data()
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(docs)

query_engine = index.as_query_engine(
    similarity_top_k=10,
    response_mode="compact"
)
print(query_engine.query("What are the key architectural decisions?"))

bytes.topic: RAG — chunking strategy, embedding, retrieval, reranking
bytes.title: Anthropic API RAG — Chunking, Voyage Embeddings, and Claude as Your Reranker
bytes.body:
Anthropic doesn't ship its own embedding model — pair Claude with Voyage AI (`voyage-3` or `voyage-code-3` for code corpora) for embeddings. Chunk at 512 tokens with ~10% overlap; Voyage's 32k input window handles aggressive context merging at retrieval time.

Claude's 200k-token context is RAG's biggest architectural lever: retrieve 20–40 chunks instead of 3–5, then let Claude synthesize across them rather than hallucinate from sparse context. For cross-encoder reranking without deploying a dedicated model, prompt Claude to score each `(query, chunk)` pair and return structured JSON. It outperforms bi-encoder rerankers on domain-specific corpora because it reads query and chunk jointly.

The real Anthropic-specific unlock: prompt caching. Pass your top-k reranked chunks as the system prompt with `cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"}`. On repeated queries against the same document set, cached input tokens cost 10× less and return ~80% faster — critical for high-QPS retrieval pipelines. Combine with `tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "retrieve"}` to force grounded generation and eliminate fabricated citations.
bytes.code_snippet:
import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def rag_with_claude(query: str, chunks: list[str]) -> str:
    scoring = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            f"Score relevance 0-10 per chunk. Return JSON: [{{\"i\":int,\"s\":int}}]\nQuery: {query}\nChunks: {json.dumps(list(enumerate(chunks)))}"
        }]
    )
    ranked = sorted(json.loads(scoring.content[0].text), key=lambda x: x["s"], reverse=True)
    top = [chunks[r["i"]] for r in ranked[:5]]

    return client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=[{"type": "text", "text": "\n\n".join(top), "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}}],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": query}]
    ).content[0].text

bytes.topic: OpenAI API — chat completions, function calling, structured outputs, embeddings
bytes.title: anthropic_api Tool Use — Forcing Structured JSON Without a Dedicated Mode
bytes.body: Unlike OpenAI's `response_format: { type: 'json_object' }`, the Anthropic API has no standalone JSON mode. The idiomatic pattern for structured outputs is tool use: define a tool with a strict `input_schema`, then set `tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": "your_tool"}` to force the model to call it. Claude's response will have `stop_reason="tool_use"` and a content block of type `tool_use` whose `input` field is already a parsed dict — no `JSON.parse` required. This is more reliable than prompt-engineering JSON because the model is constrained by schema at the sampling level. For chat completions, `client.messages.create()` is the sole entry point — there's no `/v1/chat/completions` path. Anthropic has no embeddings endpoint; use Voyage AI (`voyage-3`) via the Anthropic-partnered SDK or directly. For multi-turn, manually append the assistant's `tool_use` block and a `tool_result` user block back into `messages` — the API is stateless and context is your responsibility.

bytes.topic: Anthropic API — Claude models, system prompts, tool use, streaming
bytes.title: Anthropic API — Streaming Tool Use Responses Without Dropping Input JSON
bytes.body: When you stream a tool use response from the Anthropic API, the tool's input JSON arrives fragmented across multiple `input_json_delta` events — not as a single block. A common bug is reading only the final `content_block_stop` and wondering why `tool_use.input` is empty. You must accumulate `delta.partial_json` across all `content_block_delta` events where `delta.type === 'input_json_delta'`, then parse the concatenated string once the block closes. Each tool call is scoped to a `content_block_start` event with `content_block.type === 'tool_use'`, which gives you the `id` and `name` upfront. Track blocks by index. The same stream can contain interleaved `text_delta` and `input_json_delta` events across different block indexes — handle both. After the stream ends, reconstruct the full `messages` array (including the assistant turn with all content blocks) before appending a `tool` role message with your function results. Skipping this causes a 400: missing assistant message in multi-turn tool loops.

bytes.topic: Groq — inference speed, supported models, API compatibility
bytes.title: Anthropic API + Groq — Mixing Fast Open-Source Inference into Your Claude Pipelines
bytes.body:
Groq's inference platform delivers ~500 tokens/sec on models like `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` and `llama-3.1-8b-instant` — roughly 10–20x faster than most hosted APIs. For Anthropic API developers, the practical use case is hybrid pipelines: route latency-sensitive, lower-stakes steps (intent classification, tool-call routing, semantic gating) to Groq, then invoke `claude.messages.create()` only where Claude's reasoning quality justifies the cost.

Groq's API is OpenAI-compatible, not Anthropic-compatible — you cannot point `anthropic.Anthropic()` at Groq's base URL. Instead, instantiate `openai.OpenAI` with `base_url='https://api.groq.com/openai/v1'`. Response shapes differ: Groq returns `choices[0].message.content` (OpenAI schema) while Anthropic returns `message.content[0].text`. Handle both explicitly — shared abstractions here will bite you in production.

Current Groq model roster: `llama-3.3-70b-versatile`, `llama-3.1-8b-instant`, `mixtral-8x7b-32768`, `gemma2-9b-it`. Use `8b-instant` for sub-100ms classifications. Reserve `claude-sonnet-4-6` or `claude-opus-4-6` for the pipeline steps where depth and instruction-following actually matter.
bytes.code_snippet:
import anthropic
from openai import OpenAI

claude = anthropic.Anthropic()
groq = OpenAI(api_key="gsk_...", base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1")

def classify_intent(text: str) -> str:
    res = groq.chat.completions.create(
        model="llama-3.1-8b-instant",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Classify intent (one word): {text}"}],
        max_tokens=10,
    )
    return res.choices[0].message.content.strip()

def generate_summary(text: str, intent: str) -> str:
    msg = claude.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=300,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"[Intent: {intent}]\n\n{text}"}],
    )
    return msg.content[0].text

bytes.topic: Ollama — local model serving, model library, privacy benefits
bytes.title: anthropic_api + Ollama — Local-First Dev Without Burning API Credits
bytes.body:
When building with the Anthropic Python SDK, every prompt costs tokens—including throwaway test calls during development. Ollama runs open-weight models (Llama 3.2, Mistral, Gemma 2) locally, and LiteLLM exposes them behind an Anthropic-compatible REST interface. Point `anthropic.Anthropic(base_url=...)` at `localhost:4000` and your existing `client.messages.create()` calls work unchanged—no API key, no latency, no data leaving your machine.

The swap is one env var: `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`. In dev, point it at LiteLLM proxying `ollama/llama3.2`. In prod, unset it and the SDK falls back to `api.anthropic.com`. Your `MessageParam` schemas, tool definitions, and system prompt logic stay identical—only the inference backend changes.

Privacy benefit is concrete: customer PII or proprietary code never reaches Anthropic's servers during iteration. Once prompts are stable, flip to `claude-sonnet-4-5` for production-quality output. The pattern also pays off in CI—spin up Ollama in a Docker service step, run your integration tests against real `messages.create()` calls, consume zero quota.
bytes.code_snippet:
import anthropic
import os

client = anthropic.Anthropic(
    api_key=os.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", "sk-local"),
    base_url=os.getenv("ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL", "https://api.anthropic.com"),
)

response = client.messages.create(
    model=os.getenv("MODEL_ID", "claude-sonnet-4-5"),
    max_tokens=512,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarize this diff: ..."}],
)
print(response.content[0].text)
# Dev:  ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=http://localhost:4000 MODEL_ID=ollama/llama3.2 python main.py
# Prod: unset ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, use real ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

bytes.topic: Embeddings — vector representations, semantic similarity, embedding model comparison
bytes.title: Anthropic API + Voyage AI — The Right Way to Do Embeddings in Claude Pipelines
bytes.body:
Claude's API has no native embeddings endpoint — Anthropic's recommended solution is Voyage AI (voyage-3, voyage-3-lite, voyage-code-3). voyage-3 produces 1024-dim L2-normalized vectors and outperforms OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large on MTEB benchmarks for most general retrieval tasks. For code search, voyage-code-3 handles mixed natural-language and code queries far better than any general model.

The standard RAG pattern for Claude: embed docs with Voyage, retrieve top-k by cosine similarity, then pass chunks to claude-sonnet-4-6 or claude-opus-4-6 for generation. For tighter context windows and lower token costs, add a reranking step with Voyage's rerank-2 before calling Claude — it narrows the retrieved set before it ever hits your token budget.

Model comparison in brief: voyage-3 is the go-to for general text retrieval; voyage-3-lite costs ~60% less with minimal quality loss for most queries; voyage-code-3 is mandatory for any code-heavy search. All three sit in the same virtual environment as the anthropic SDK — complementary API keys, zero friction.
bytes.code_snippet:
import voyageai
import anthropic
import numpy as np

vo = voyageai.Client()  # VOYAGE_API_KEY env var
client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var

docs = ["Claude 3.5 uses extended thinking", "Llama 3 is fully open-weights"]
query = "Which model exposes its reasoning chain?"

doc_embeddings = np.array(vo.embed(docs, model="voyage-3").embeddings)
query_embedding = np.array(vo.embed([query], model="voyage-3").embeddings[0])

scores = doc_embeddings @ query_embedding  # vectors are L2-normalized
top_doc = docs[scores.argmax()]

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=256,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Context: {top_doc}\n\n{query}"}]
)
print(response.content[0].text)

bytes.topic: Fine-tuning — when to fine-tune vs prompt engineer, LoRA, QLoRA
bytes.title: anthropic_api Fine-Tuning — Claude Has No Fine-Tune Endpoint, Here's What Works Instead
bytes.body:
Claude's API exposes no fine-tuning endpoint — intentionally. Anthropic's bet: a well-prompted claude-opus-4-5 or claude-sonnet-4-5 outperforms a fine-tuned 7B on nearly every NLP task without the ops cost of LoRA/QLoRA training runs, GPU infrastructure, or checkpoint management.

When the instinct is 'I need fine-tuning,' exhaust these levers first:

**System prompt as implicit fine-tune.** A dense, role-specific system prompt (persona, output schema, negative examples, edge case handling) shifts the distribution dramatically. Treat it as a frozen adapter layer.

**Few-shot examples in `messages[]`.** Inject 3–8 `user`/`assistant` pairs before the real turn. This is in-context LoRA — no gradient descent, immediate iteration.

**Assistant prefill.** Pass a partial `assistant` message to force output format. Combined with few-shot, this eliminates JSON-schema drift without instruction fine-tuning.

**Extended thinking for complex reasoning.** `thinking: { type: 'enabled', budget_tokens: 8000 }` on claude-sonnet-4-5 replaces chain-of-thought fine-tuning for multi-step tasks.

Reach for LoRA/QLoRA on an open-source model only when: latency SLA < 200ms, data is proprietary and can't leave your VPC, or cost per million tokens is genuinely prohibitive at scale.

bytes.topic: Agents & Tool Use — function calling, ReAct pattern, tool selection, agent loops
bytes.title: Anthropic API Agent Loops — Building Reliable Tool Use Cycles
bytes.body:
Anthropic's tool use drives agent loops through a strict message protocol. When Claude decides to call a tool, `response.stop_reason` is `"tool_use"` — not `"end_turn"`. Your loop runs until `stop_reason === "end_turn"`, at which point Claude has synthesized a final answer.

The critical mechanic: after a `tool_use` block arrives in `response.content`, execute the tool and append two messages — the assistant's raw content (preserving the `tool_use` block) and a user message containing a `tool_result` block with the matching `tool_use_id`. Mismatched IDs trigger hard validation errors.

Control tool selection with `tool_choice`: `{"type": "auto"}` lets Claude decide, `{"type": "any"}` forces at least one tool call (ideal for structured extraction), and `{"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}` pins a specific tool. For ReAct-style reasoning, enable extended thinking — Claude's `thinking` blocks are preserved across turns inside `response.content`, giving you observable scratchpad state.

Multi-tool turns are fully supported: a single response can contain multiple `tool_use` blocks. Batch all results into one `tool_result` user message before the next API call.
bytes.code_snippet:
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
tools = [{"name": "get_weather", "description": "Get weather for a city", "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"city": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["city"]}}]
messages = [{"role": "user", "content": "What's the weather in Tokyo and Berlin?"}]

while True:
    response = client.messages.create(model="claude-opus-4-6", max_tokens=1024, tools=tools, messages=messages)
    if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
        print(response.content[0].text)
        break
    tool_results = []
    for block in response.content:
        if block.type == "tool_use":
            result = call_tool(block.name, block.input)  # your dispatch logic
            tool_results.append({"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": block.id, "content": result})
    messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
    messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

bytes.topic: Streaming — streaming responses, token-by-token output, UX patterns
bytes.title: Anthropic API Streaming — Token-by-Token Output and UX Patterns That Actually Matter
bytes.body:
Anthropic's SDK exposes two streaming paths: the low-level `create(stream=True)` that returns raw `MessageStreamEvent` objects, and the higher-level `client.messages.stream()` context manager that wraps them into ergonomic helpers. Prefer the latter.

Inside the context manager, `stream.text_stream` is a synchronous iterator that yields decoded text deltas one token at a time — perfect for piping directly to `print(..., flush=True)` or buffering into a frontend SSE channel. Under the hood it surfaces `content_block_delta` events where `delta.type == "text_delta"`.

After the stream closes, `stream.get_final_message()` returns the fully assembled `Message` object including `usage.input_tokens` and `usage.output_tokens` — so you don't lose billing data mid-stream.

For UX, don't debounce on the server side. Flush every token and let the client control render cadence. In Next.js, pair Anthropic's SSE with a `ReadableStream` + `TextEncoder` to forward tokens directly to the browser without buffering — this cuts perceived latency by 60–80% on long responses. Always set `max_tokens` explicitly; streaming doesn't relax that limit.
bytes.code_snippet:
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-opus-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain RLHF in 3 sentences."}],
) as stream:
    for text in stream.text_stream:
        print(text, end="", flush=True)

final = stream.get_final_message()
print(f"\nTokens: {final.usage.input_tokens} in / {final.usage.output_tokens} out")

bytes.topic: Cost Management — token counting, caching, batching, model selection for cost
bytes.title: Anthropic API Cost Management — Token Counting, Caching, and Batching
bytes.body: Three levers cut Anthropic API costs significantly. First, pre-flight token counting: `client.messages.count_tokens()` accepts the same payload as `messages.create()` and returns exact input token counts before you commit to a request — use this to gate expensive calls or enforce budget limits per user. Second, prompt caching: mark static content (system prompts, large documents, few-shot examples) with `cache_control: { type: 'ephemeral' }`. Cached tokens cost 10% of base input price on reads and 25% on writes, with a 5-minute TTL that resets on each cache hit. For a 10K-token system prompt hit 100 times per hour, that's a 90% reduction on those tokens alone. Third, the Message Batches API (`client.messages.batches.create()`) processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost — ideal for classification, embedding prep, or offline summarization. For model selection: Haiku 3.5 handles retrieval filtering and intent classification; Sonnet 4 owns most production tasks; Opus 4 only when multi-step reasoning or accuracy is non-negotiable. Never default to Opus.

bytes.topic: Evaluation — LLM-as-judge, RAGAS for RAG evaluation, benchmarks
bytes.title: anthropic_api — LLM-as-Judge, RAGAS, and Batch Benchmarking
bytes.body:
Claude's Messages API makes a reliable LLM-as-judge backbone. The key is a structured system prompt that enforces JSON scoring — claude-sonnet-4-6 follows strict output schemas consistently. Always set temperature=0 for deterministic, reproducible scores across eval runs.

For RAG pipelines, RAGAS integrates via its LangchainLLMWrapper using langchain-anthropic, giving you faithfulness, answer relevancy, and context precision metrics powered by the same Claude model driving your RAG stack — removing cross-model scoring bias.

For custom evals, parse scores directly from response.content[0].text. When benchmarking haiku vs sonnet, track response.usage.input_tokens and wall-clock latency per prompt to build cost/quality Pareto curves — critical for choosing the right model tier per eval category.

For large benchmark suites (MMLU-style, 1,000+ prompts), use client.messages.batches.create() — the Batch API handles up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction. Pass a custom_id per eval item so you can join results back to ground-truth labels without index juggling.
bytes.code_snippet:
import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def llm_judge(question: str, context: str, answer: str) -> dict:
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=256,
        temperature=0,
        system='Score this RAG answer. Return ONLY JSON: {"faithfulness": 0.0-1.0, "relevance": 0.0-1.0, "reason": "string"}',
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Q: {question}\nCtx: {context}\nA: {answer}"}],
    )
    return json.loads(msg.content[0].text)

bytes.topic: Safety & Guardrails — output filtering, prompt injection, hallucination mitigation
bytes.title: Anthropic API Safety & Guardrails — Prompt Injection Defense, Output Filtering, and Hallucination Mitigation
bytes.body: Anthropic's API gives you several layers to harden production pipelines. Against prompt injection, use a hardened system prompt with explicit role boundaries — prepend `<system>` instructions that tell Claude to ignore instructions embedded in user-supplied content, and never interpolate raw user input directly into the system turn. For output filtering, combine `stop_sequences` to terminate on known-bad tokens with a post-response validation pass using a lightweight second `messages.create` call that asks Claude to classify its own output as safe/unsafe — cheap at `haiku` tier. For hallucination mitigation, ground responses in retrieved context using the `documents` pattern inside the user turn: wrap each source in `<document>` tags with a `<source>` attribute, then instruct Claude to cite by source ID and refuse to answer if evidence is absent. Use `temperature: 0` for factual queries to reduce stochastic drift. Finally, enable streaming with `stream=True` and validate partial token windows in real time so you can abort before a problematic completion fully lands — far cheaper than post-hoc moderation.

bytes.topic: Context Management — long context handling, chunking, sliding window, summarization
bytes.title: Anthropic API — Taming Long Contexts with Chunking, Sliding Windows, and Summarization
bytes.body:
Claude supports up to 200K input tokens, but blindly stuffing all context into every request burns cost and degrades coherence on ultra-long inputs. The right strategy depends on your access pattern.

For document Q&A, chunk your corpus into ~4K-token segments with a 10–15% overlap, embed each chunk, retrieve top-k by cosine similarity, then pass only those chunks in the `user` turn. This keeps your prompt under 8–12K tokens for most queries.

For multi-turn conversations, implement a sliding window: keep the last N messages and prepend a rolling summary in the `system` prompt. When the window exceeds a threshold, call Claude once with `max_tokens` capped low just to produce the summary, then flush old turns.

Use `client.messages.count_tokens()` (beta) to measure prompt size before sending — it returns `input_tokens` without consuming output quota. Gate your chunking logic on that count rather than guessing character length.

For retrieval-heavy pipelines, combine both: RAG for the knowledge base, sliding window for conversation history. Claude's extended context is a safety net, not a design strategy.
bytes.code_snippet:
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
MAX_HISTORY_TOKENS = 40_000

def trim_history(messages: list, system: str) -> list:
    token_count = client.beta.messages.count_tokens(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        system=system,
        messages=messages,
        betas=["token-counting-2024-11-01"],
    ).input_tokens

    while token_count > MAX_HISTORY_TOKENS and len(messages) > 2:
        summary_resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=512,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Summarize this conversation in 3 sentences:\n{messages[:4]}"}],
        )
        summary = summary_resp.content[0].text
        messages = [{"role": "assistant", "content": f"[Earlier summary]: {summary}"}] + messages[4:]
        token_count = client.beta.messages.count_tokens(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            system=system,
            messages=messages,
            betas=["token-counting-2024-11-01"],
        ).input_tokens
    return messages
